---
title: "research"
format: html
---

---
title: "Research"
---

## The Masters Portfolio

Virginia Tech's Masters of History program asks students to produce three 
substantial works: a historiography, a journal article, and a third project 
of choice. My portfolio uses these three forms to trace the legal, spiritual, 
and resistance histories of Peyote religions — bringing together my background 
in Indigenous history with new work in legal, drug, and resistance history. 

## Journal Article: The Long Peyote Road: A History of Peyotism and Indigenous Resistance

My primary research examines the history of Peyote religions among Indigenous 
peoples of North America — a history stretching back over six thousand years. 
Despite sustained colonial efforts to suppress Indigenous medicines and spiritual 
practices, the use of Peyote has persisted and spread through processes of 
cultural exchange, adaptation, and resistance.

At the intersection of Indigenous history, legal history, religious history, 
and the history of the War on Drugs, my work traces what I call a 
**"repertoire of resistance"** — a set of tools and strategies that Peyotist 
groups have developed and deployed to resist colonization. I argue that this 
repertoire is not fixed to any single community, but can be adapted and adopted 
by other groups facing colonial or governmental suppression.

## History TikTok Videos of Resistance History in Virginia

The other section of the portfolio is a public history outreach program to bring Indigenous
history to high-school age students. These are deeply researched topics that are ajacent to 
historic moments that students likely are already familiar with. I bring students back to 
Jamestown, World War II, Integration (in the 1950s-1970s) and connect those histories to now.
These videos bring the same tools from the journal article, but localize them here in Virginia.
I hope that these videos can spark some interest in local, Indigenous, and resistance history.

## Research Interests

- Indigenous histories of what is now the United States
- The history of the War on Drugs
- Entheogenic plants and their cultural and spiritual histories
- Histories of resistance and anti-colonial struggle
- Legal history and environmental history
Are you working on anything else research-wise right now?
What drew you to these topics — Indigenous history, the War on Drugs, entheogens, resistance?
